cards.rule: When converting a statement from Direct to Indirect Speech, change the reporting verb 'said' to 'said that' or 'told + object'. Remove the inverted commas and shift the tense one step back (backshift).
cards.example:
Direct: He said, "I am happy."
Indirect: He said that he was happy.
cards.practice: Convert to Indirect Speech: She said, "I live in Dhaka."

cards.rule: For Yes/No questions, use 'asked + whether/if' as the reporting phrase. The question becomes a statement (subject before verb), and the question mark is removed.
cards.example:
Direct: He said to me, "Are you tired?"
Indirect: He asked me whether/if I was tired.
cards.practice: Convert: The teacher said to Rafi, "Did you do your homework?"

cards.rule: For Wh-questions, use 'asked + wh-word' to connect the clauses. The question becomes a statement word order (subject + verb), and the question mark is dropped.
cards.example:
Direct: She said to him, "Where do you work?"
Indirect: She asked him where he worked.
cards.practice: Convert: He said to me, "What time is it?"

cards.rule: Commands use 'told/ordered/requested + object + to + verb'. The imperative verb becomes a to-infinitive. For negative commands, use 'told + not + to + verb'.
cards.example:
Direct: The teacher said to the student, "Open your book."
Indirect: The teacher told the student to open his book.
cards.practice: Convert: Mother said to me, "Don't waste your time."

cards.rule: Exclamations and wishes use verbs like 'exclaimed', 'wished', or 'prayed'. The exclamatory sentence changes to a statement, and the exclamation mark is removed.
cards.example:
Direct: He said, "What a beautiful scene!"
Indirect: He exclaimed that it was a very beautiful scene.
cards.practice: Convert: She said, "How kind you are!"

cards.rule: In Indirect Speech, pronouns change according to the subject and object of the reporting clause. First person pronouns follow the subject; second person follows the object; third person stays the same.
cards.example:
Direct: He said, "I love my country."
Indirect: He said that he loved his country.
(First person 'I/my' → 'he/his' following reporting subject)
cards.practice: Identify the pronoun changes: She said to him, "I will help you tomorrow."

cards.rule: When the reporting verb is in past tense, shift the quoted verb one step back: Present Simple → Past Simple, Present Continuous → Past Continuous, Present Perfect → Past Perfect, Past Simple → Past Perfect.
cards.example:
Direct: "I am reading a book." (Present Continuous)
Indirect: He said he was reading a book. (Past Continuous)
cards.practice: Apply backshift: She said, "I have already eaten lunch."

cards.subtopic: Time & Place Expressions in Indirect Speech
cards.rule: Time and place adverbs shift in Indirect Speech: 'now' → 'then', 'today' → 'that day', 'tomorrow' → 'the next day', 'yesterday' → 'the day before', 'here' → 'there', 'this' → 'that'.
cards.example:
Direct: He said, "I will come here tomorrow."
Indirect: He said that he would go there the next day.
cards.practice: Convert with correct time/place words: She said, "I bought this dress yesterday."

cards.rule: To convert Indirect to Direct Speech: restore inverted commas, change 'said that' → 'said', reverse pronoun changes, reverse tense backshift, and restore original time/place words.
cards.example:
Indirect: He told me that he was going to Dhaka that day.
Direct: He said to me, "I am going to Dhaka today."
cards.practice: Convert to Direct Speech: She asked him if he had finished his work.

cards.rule: Modal verbs also shift in Indirect Speech: 'will' → 'would', 'can' → 'could', 'may' → 'might', 'shall' → 'should'. 'Must', 'ought to', 'used to', and 'would' usually remain unchanged.
cards.example:
Direct: She said, "I will finish this work."
Indirect: She said that she would finish that work.
cards.practice: Convert: He said, "I can solve this problem."
